sentences.text: The only welcome that we got them in Bergen-Belsen is [? floormen. ?] We came off the train-- I don't know how many people we were.
A hundred-- thousands, I would say.
And we had to go to through a line where there were kapos.
And blockaltestes, the white stripes, the leadership from the camps.
At first they hold us up.
They were in the wagons when we came from Harzug to Bergen-Belsen.
We within ourselves, we had in the wagons, closed wagons.
So they killed a couple of the blockaltestes and the kapos.
Where they were hitting, and punishing, and they're killing people in the camp.
Not-- I am not talking about the Nazis.
I'm talking now the Pollacks, and then maybe some even Jews between us.
And some Germans, but they were prisoners of war.
In the-- in the-- in the-- inside locked wagons, they killed them.
And when we arrived, they told the other kapos in Bergen-Belsen, the other leaders, about what we did.
So-- they put themselves up on the line, and it stinks, and whatnot.
And we had to [INAUDIBLE] to do them.
And they killed-- hit them on the head, and kicked them.
All from our people when they arrived to Bergen-Belsen into the other side.
Until then, we were then over to the other side.
And I know, I got-- I got-- hit in the head.
A mark here in the head with the stick.
No, we never-- no contact whatsoever.
In the camps where I was, no women whatsoever.
We never saw women in the camps.
And same like my sister was in camp where she died.
It was not a question from sexual activities.
The question was from survival, from eating.
I mean, to put it in your mouth, that's water.
We didn't have enough water to survive.
They put me to work in Bergen-Belsen.
After they-- pulling dead bodies into a big grave.
You know, a grave was made with a bulldozer.
And I couldn't pull a body.
So four of us, we, with our strength, we tied to the feet.
We have-- two strings to both feets.
And four of us pulled them a half a kilometer to the-- or whatever the bodies were.
And just throw the body into the grave.
And-- now, we kept on for pulling them.
The peoples just kept on dying.
We kept on pulling, and people kept on dying-- day and night.
Then in Bergen-Belsen, they had another section on the other side.
You could have seen-- far away, where was straight ground-- women.
They had a women's camp at Bergen-Belsen.
That's the only camp what I know of-- I mean, when I was-- where they had women in the concentration camp on the other side in that camp.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was going on for a few weeks.
One night-- one night, that the hunger was so, so, terrible.
Once a day, they gave a little soup.
Water was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went over that, once night.
One day, in the middle of the day, we jumped over the fence to a kitchen.
There were four or six of us.
And one of my friends got shot in the foot.
And that-- I jumped over with a few potatoes.
Saved-- we have like a group.
He went into the fat [INAUDIBLE] and the meat here.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] You can't-- speak about it.
You can put it on paper what Bergen-Belsen was.
One night, I felt like-- we walked around like scavengers in the night that evening.
And all of a sudden, I smelled-- smell like a-- like a dog smells.
So we walked around also for four, six of us.
And they were cooking a potato, one place I'm at-- whatever.
And in one place, one barrack had a little fire.
And they let us-- they just-- die.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I say him, you know, it smells like meat.
And we go over closer to a group, a group of people there.
And we got beaten just to get away for there, you know.
We were-- in my age, you know.
In the morning, I found out that group were cannibal.
They were eating-- they were eating, cooking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] human.
It was a doctor from France.
And there was some other two doctors.
They were eat-- cooking from the men-- men liver from the men.
Cooking and then-- I beg your pardon?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't need a surgeon knife.
And they-- and how did I found out?
In the evening, in the morning.
And they took them, and they killed them.
Still they-- the-- our own people.
Took these guys-- I don't know how many of them were involved-- and killed them right on the spot.
I don't think they were-- Jewish people-- I didn't know.
They found bodies the next day opened, taken out.
And I know this is what happened.
And then they killed these people what they did.
Number one-- at least in other camps, you had one meal.
You had a soup or they-- you got half a piece of bread.
At Bergen-Belsen, you practically get nothing.
They didn't put you to work.
And I didn't go out to a [NON-ENGLISH] to work.
They put you in to die.
Practically, to die the last-- the last-- of several months, or several weeks, or whatever.
Some of them, they put to work to clean or to schlep the bodies like I schlepped the bodies.
And you saw people going not-- and not-- loosing everything.
I think it had a lot to do with belief and HaShem, as far as I'm concerned.
With-- I-- after the war or--
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Yeah, it was discussed with my friends.
As long as I had my friend, Yankev Hirsch.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and God will help me.
From-- from Dyhernfurth I made friends.
And we survived-- from Funfteichen I-- from Harzug.
And we went for potatoes, and try to share it together, and so on, and so forth.
That's an-- oh, that's an interesting thing what happened.
I'm in the clothing line all the year.
It was in-- I started in the United States.
And the imports, clothing, and I started small, and always made a living on it.
And I had one clothing store on Grand Street on Lower East Side.
And I had a couple of people working for me.
I had a tailor, and I had three, four salesman.
And one day, I'm at the register, and I didn't say, I'm the boss.
The business was running while I had other two stores running.
Running from one store to the other.
And I'm the store, and a man walks in.
And he's trying on a shirt, a suit with another friend.
And the salesman talks to him, and I'll do this for you, and I'll do that for you, I'll make it a little shorter, I'll make fix it.
So the one suit is going to sell him another suit.
I turn around, I look at him.
I let him finish, and I go over.
They said the man would talk to the [GERMAN] in and Dyhernfurth.
And maybe saved my life with his son to go into the work as a schlosser.
Fishov, you know who I am here?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't say anything else.
And he thought me the words.
Yeah, I repeated this to him.
Moishe is now here in Brooklyn.
And he, what are you doing?
He was the manager for Barton's Candy.
Pop man-- managing, and so on, and so forth.
You asked me if I met somebody from--
Then Moishe came to see me.
And he brought his friends, and I met his wife.
And then I met another person from big camp.
I want for a trip to Israel in 1960.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I looked up for survivors from the concentration camp, the list, and so on.
And I asked the [NON-ENGLISH] to find out what's-- who's alive, who from the town, and so on.
And one guy told me, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is alive.
We made a [INAUDIBLE], took a taxi.
A day later, one day later, the Russians surrounded the camp.
Instead they shall burn us, they surrounded the camp, and they didn't get a chance.
And the whole hospital got-- I mean, the people in it got liberated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my people, people that I met after the camp.
We just saw one-- the day and the evening-- that Germans are pulling out slowly.
They leave only one here, one there, one there on the guard.
Only here and there are guards.
You know, we didn't know what's going on.
And in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] English row of tanks marched into the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I should say it.
The English captain came out of the tank.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, who speaks English?
We were 95% we were Jewish people from Poland, from Czechoslovakia, from Hungary, from Romania, from Lithuania, from Bulgaria, from France.
I don't know why-- German Jews I counted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A little Polish Jew from Warsaw stood up.
He spoke a little bit of English.
SO the first thing he asked him, what nationality we are.
We what-- hundred thousands, thousands, hundreds of people, whatever around the tanks.
He didn't know what mean you have the yellow thing here.
So he says, Hitler let live so many Jews?
So the Polish Jew says, I mean the translator, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
He makes believe that he's English.
They want to see we're going to react.
And we all moved back a couple of-- quite a few feet from the tanks and we got scared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they start talking.
And then we saw it's not so.
One individual captain in the summit.
I wouldn't know if I see it.
I didn't see too much soap anywhere.
And that was quite a-- and-- and in Bergen-Belsen, whatever was left of the survivors-- after all the kills and all the death in that camp-- well, they blow them from all over in that camp.
I believe the army, that captain, or that army whoever came in killed half of the population.

Any intelligent-- any man with a little bit of sense, any common sense will not go ahead and give for the population what didn't eat, and didn't drink, and didn't have the typhus, and sickness, and lice.
And they're falling, they're running from them from front and from back.
Goes ahead and gives them that day when they came in-- a kilo [INAUDIBLE] fat meat what I had and with a whole bread.
When they gave this for the population in Bergen-Belsen, who ever gave the order to give it to them, killed half of the population.
They didn't think about what they needed.
Accidental or deliberately, I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do not know.
Well, if they wouldn't have give that, and after that it started out.
Next day, you saw people falling like flies after they ate it.
And I was-- and I believe I was-- I had some friends, they survived.
Kept me-- one helped the other.
Made hot tea, hot water, hot whatever.
I think I was under typhus a couple of days.
They didn't say-- yeah, go ahead.
They didn't come into camps, the English, and say, you're free.
We knew automatically a sense that we were free.
I mean, the camps were liberated.
They can't-- that means that you can go.
So they had to-- the trains were bombed.
And they start talking to go back home.
No trains, no buses, no transportation.
They were there, I mean, in a way.
I didn't see-- in the camps?
Now, they did come in a week later.
Or two weeks later came in organizations from different part of the world from Sweden, from the United States to help.
Now, what I did there-- we went to the magazines.
We took off the striped clothing.
We changed clothing with our clothing.
They had sheets, pillowcases, and we took.
I remember I went in with two of my friends to the town-- village, German village nearby.
And I begged for a couple of eggs.
Eggs or a piece of bread, fresh bread, you know.
And this is what I ate to survive.
A lot of them closed their doors when they saw us coming.
Shut the doors and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about-- I mean, look.
Just hanging around and trying to survive.
And then they were talking about-- it started up-- by the way, after came the [NON-ENGLISH], and organization.
And they took people to Sweden.
Sick people, typhus people to get them better.
And they took them on the train from Bergen-Belsen, put them on the train, and the train went to Lubeck, in Germany.
And the boats took them over to Sweden.
I-- I-- went ahead and I had my teeth-- my teeths were knocked out.
I remember, I went into that village.
And I decided that time that I'm going to go instead to try to schlep my bag go home, and go to Sweden.
In Sweden, they were talking [INAUDIBLE] they want in the war.
And they try to make good for the people, what they went through the war.
[INAUDIBLE] And I went on the to the train.
And as soon as I came to the train to the English.
They took a look at me.
They didn't let me walk out.
I thought I hear they're holding me.
And they put me on the stretcher.
And they put me on the stretcher on the train.
They took me down from this-- from the train on the stretcher.
And they put me in the boat.
In a-- like a thing, you know?
It took only a day or a night into Goteborg.
Well, I got to know some people there also.
I got to know some people who were in Goteborg in the hospital.
They took us to a hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On that hospital I was four or six weeks.
Oh, they took care of me.
First of all, they saved me from top to bottom.
On-- you know, and scrubbed me.
And put me in a clean bed like this.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I got the lices.
That-- I have-- you know, you say, shaving.
In Dyhernfurth, in the concentration camp-- I reminded myself with shaving.
Twice a week, they shaved my head in the middle, in case if I escape.
Everybody was shaved twice a week.
